summary: Cathy O'Neil shares her experience preparing for bariatric surgery, describing her strict pre-operative liquid diet and an unexpected health scare from sodium deficiency. She discovered that the 'meal replacement' drinks prescribed didn't contain adequate salt, causing weakness and disorientation despite her careful compliance. After researching online forums, she found that pre-op diet protocols vary widely and many patients don't follow them strictly. The post blends personal medical narrative with her characteristic skepticism about supposedly authoritative guidelines.
key_points: The prescribed meal replacement drinks claiming to provide complete nutrition were actually deficient in sodium, causing dangerous symptoms
Self-directed research and problem-solving (adding chicken broth) resolved what strict protocol compliance had caused
Online forums revealed wide variation in pre-op diet protocols and widespread non-compliance among patients
Following medical directions 'carefully' can sometimes make you sick when those directions are flawed
There's a spectrum of non-compliance from reasonable modifications to dangerous behavior like eating sandwiches before surgery
money_quotes: It's upsetting that I was making myself sick by following directions carefully.
I modified my diet to include chicken broth and now I feel perfectly fine, but it made me wonder how the directions could be so badly off.
They're very disgusting, being chalky and sickeningly sweet, but I've been extremely diligent, learning to drink them quickly and try not to gag.
nobody, or at least very few people, seem to actually follow these diets
some people are hilariously bad at following their diet
except for the crazy people who are eating sandwiches right before surgery and planning not to tell their surgeon
key_insight: Even when you follow medical protocols diligently, the protocols themselves may be flawed, requiring patients to become their own advocates and problem-solvers.
